volumeTitle: Children of Heracles. Hippolytus. Andromache. Hecuba
label: Children of Heracles (Section 795 - Section 980)

sections.label: Section 795
sections.original: τί δʼ ἔστι ; μῶν τι κεδνὸν ἠγωνίζετο ;
sections.translation: How so ? Did he perform some deed of prowess ?

sections.label: Section 796
sections.original: νέος μεθέστηκʼ ἐκ γέροντος αὖθις αὖ .
sections.translation: He hath passed from age to youth once more .

sections.label: Section 797
sections.original: θαυμάστʼ ἔλεξας · ἀλλά σʼ εὐτυχῆ φίλων
sections.translation: Thy tale is passing strange ; but first I would that thou shouldst tell me , how our friends won the day .

sections.label: Section 799
sections.original: εἷς μου λόγος σοι πάντα σημανεῖ τάδε .
sections.translation: One speech of mine puts it all clearly before thee .

sections.label: Section 800
sections.original: ἐπεὶ γὰρ ἀλλήλοισιν ὁπλίτην στρατὸν
sections.translation: When we had deployed our troops and marshalled them face to face with one another , Hyllus dismounted from his four-horsed chariot and stood midway betwixt the hosts . Then cried he , Captain , who art come from Argos ,

sections.label: Section 805
sections.original: ἥκεις , τί τήνδε γαῖαν οὐκ εἰάσαμεν
sections.translation: why cannot we leave this land alone ? No hurt wilt thou do Mycenae , if of one man thou rob her ; come ! meet me in single combat , and , if thou slay me , take the children of Heracles away with thee ,

sections.label: Section 810
sections.original: τιμὰς πατρῴους καὶ δόμους ἔχειν ἄφες .
sections.translation: but , if thou fall , leave me to possess my ancestral honours and my home . The host cried yes ! saying the scheme he offered was a fair one , both to rid them of their trouble and satisfy their valour . But that other , feeling no shame before those who heard the challenge or at his own cowardice , quailed , general though he was ,

sections.label: Section 815
sections.original: ἐλθεῖν ἐτόλμησʼ ἐγγὺς ἀλκίμου δορός ,
sections.translation: to come within reach of the stubborn spear , showing himself an abject coward ; yet with such a spirit he came to enslave the children of Heracles . Then did Hyllus withdraw to his own ranks again , and the prophets seeing that no reconciliation would be effected by single combat ,

sections.label: Section 820
sections.original: διαλλαγὰς ἔγνωσαν οὐ τελουμένας ,
sections.translation: began the sacrifice without delay and forthwith let flow from a human [*] throat auspicious streams of blood . And some were mounting chariots , while others couched beneath the shelter of their shields , and the king of the Athenians ,

sections.label: Section 825
sections.original: στρατῷ παρήγγελλ ’ οἷα χρὴ τὸν εὐγενῆ ·
sections.translation: as a highborn chieftain should , would exhort his host : Fellow-citizens , the land , that feeds you and that gave you birth , demands to-day the help of every man . Likewise Eurystheus besought his allies that they should scorn to sully the feme of Argos and Mycenae .

sections.label: Section 830
sections.original: ἐπεὶ δʼ ἐσήμηνʼ ὄρθιον Τυρσηνικῇ
sections.translation: Anon the Etrurian trumpet sounded loud and clear , and hand to hand they rushed ; then think how loudly clashed their ringing shields , what din arose of cries and groans confused ! At first the onset of the Argive spearmen

sections.label: Section 835
sections.original: ἐρρήξαθʼ ἡμᾶς · εἶτʼ ἐχώρησαν πάλιν .
sections.translation: broke our ranks ; then they in turn gave ground ; next , foot to foot and man to man , they fought their stubborn fray , many falling the while . And either chief cheered on his men , Sons of Athens ! Ye who till the fields of Argos !

sections.label: Section 840
sections.original: σπείροντες — οὐκ ἀρήξετʼ αἰσχύνην πόλει ;
sections.translation: ward from your land disgrace . Do all we could , and spite of every effort , scarce could we turn the Argive line in flight . When lo ! old Iolaus sees Hyllus starting from the ranks , whereon he lifts his hands to him with a prayer

sections.label: Section 845
sections.original: Ἰόλαος ἐμβῆσαί νιν ἵππειον δίφρον .
sections.translation: to take him up into his chariot . Thereon he seized the reins and went hard after the horses of Eurystheus . From this point onward must I speak from hearsay , though hitherto as one whose own eyes saw . For as he was crossing Pallene ’ s hill ,

sections.label: Section 850
sections.original: δίας Ἀθάνας , ἅρμʼ ἰδὼν Εὐρυσθέως ,
sections.translation: sacred to the goddess Athene , he caught sight of Eurystheus ’ chariot , and prayed to Hebe and to Zeus , that for one single day he might grow young again and wreak his vengeance on his foes . Now must thou hear a wondrous tale : two stars settled on the horses ’ yokes

sections.label: Section 855
sections.original: σταθέντʼ ἔκρυψαν ἅρμα λυγαίῳ νέφει ·
sections.translation: and threw the chariot into dark shadow , which — at least so say our wiser folk — were thy son and Hebe ; and from that murky gloom appeared that aged man in the form of a youth with strong young arms ; then by the rocks of Sciron

sections.label: Section 860
sections.original: τέτρωρον ἅρμα πρὸς πέτραις Σκιρωνίσιν ,
sections.translation: the hero Iolaus o ’ ertakes Eurystheus ’ chariot . And he bound his hands with gyves , and is bringing that chieftain once so prosperous as a trophy hither , whose fortune now doth preach a lesson , clear as day , to all the sons of men ,

sections.label: Section 865
sections.original: τὸν εὐτυχεῖν δοκοῦντα μὴ ζηλοῦν , πρὶν ἂν
sections.translation: that none should envy him , who seems to thrive , until they see his death ; for fortune ’ s moods last but a day .

sections.label: Section 867
sections.original: ὦ Ζεῦ τροπαῖε , νῦν ἐμοὶ δεινοῦ φόβου
sections.translation: O Zeus , who puttest my foes to flight , now may I behold the day that frees me from cruel fear !

sections.label: Section 869
sections.original: ὦ Ζεῦ , χρόνῳ μὲν τἄμ ’ ἐπεσκέψω κακά ,
sections.translation: At last , O Zeus , hast thou turned a favouring eye on my affliction ;

sections.label: Section 870
sections.original: χάριν δʼ ὅμως σοι τῶν πεπραγμένων ἔχω ·
sections.translation: yet do I thank thee for what has happened . And though ere this I did not believe my son was gathered to the gods , now am I convinced thereof . My children , now at last from toil shall ye be free , free from him , whom hideous death awaits ,

sections.label: Section 875
sections.original: Εὐρυσθέως ἔσεσθε καὶ πόλιν πατρὸς
sections.translation: Eurystheus ; now shall ye behold your father ’ s city , and set foot in the land of your inheritance , and sacrifice to those ancestral gods , from whom ye have been debarred and forced to lead in strangers ’ lands a life of wretched vagrancy . But tell me , what sage purpose Iolaus

sections.label: Section 880
sections.original: Εὐρυσθέως ἐφείσαθʼ ὥστε μὴ κτανεῖν ;
sections.translation: nursed in his heart , that he spared the life of Eurystheus , for to my mind this is no wisdom , to catch a foe and wreak no vengeance on him .

sections.label: Section 883
sections.original: τὸ σὸν προτιμῶν , ὥς νιν ὀφθαλμοῖς ἴδοις
sections.translation: ’ Twas his regard for thee , that thou might ’ st see him subject to thy hand , and triumph [*] o ’ er him .

sections.label: Section 885
sections.original: οὐ μὴν ἑκόντα γʼ αὐτόν , ἀλλὰ πρὸς βίαν
sections.translation: Rest assured , ’ twas no willing prisoner he made , but by strong constraint he bound him , for Eurystheus was loth indeed to come alive into thy presence and pay his penalty . Farewell , my aged mistress ; I pray thee remember thy first promise when I was beginning my story ;

sections.label: Section 890
sections.original: ἐλευθερώσειν μʼ · ἐν δὲ τοῖς τοιοῖσδε χρὴ
sections.translation: set me free ; for , at such a time as this , sincerity becometh noble lips . [ Exit Servant .

sections.label: Section 1.892
sections.original: Ἐμοὶ χορὸς μὲν ἡδύς , εἰ λίγεια λω-
sections.translation: Sweet is the dance to me , whenso the clear-toned flute and lovely Aphrodite shed grace upon the feast ;

sections.label: Section 1.895
sections.original: τα · τερπνὸν δέ τι καὶ φίλων ἆρʼ
sections.translation: and a joyful thing too it is , I trow , to witness the good luck of friends , who till then ne ’ er dreamt of it . For numerous is the offspring of Fate ,

sections.label: Section 1.900
sections.original: τειρʼ Αἰών τε Χρόνου παῖς .
sections.translation: that bringeth all to pass , and of Time , the son of Cronos .

sections.label: Section 1.901
sections.original: ἔχεις ὁδόν τινʼ , ὦ πόλις , δίκαιον — οὐ
sections.translation: Thine is the path of justice , O my city ; this must no man wrest from thee , thy reverence for the gods , and , whoso denieth it of thee , draws nigh to frenzy ’ s goal ,

sections.label: Section 1.905
sections.original: δεικνυμένων ἐλέγχων
sections.translation: with these plain proofs in view . Yea , for the god proclaims it clearly , by cutting short the bad man ’ s pride in every case .

sections.label: Section 2.910
sections.original: ἔστιν ἐν οὐρανῷ βεβα-
sections.translation: In heaven , mother , lives thy son , passed from earth away ; that he went down to Hades ’ halls , his body burnt by the fire ’ s fierce flame , is past belief ;

sections.label: Section 2.915
sections.original: Ἥβας τʼ ἐρατὸν χροΐζει
sections.translation: in golden halls reclined he has to wife Hebe , lovely nymph . Thou , O Hymen , hast honoured them , children both of Zeus .

sections.label: Section 2.919
sections.original: συμφέρεται τὰ πολλὰ πολ-
sections.translation: Things for the most part form a single chain ;

sections.label: Section 2.920
sections.original: λοῖς · καὶ γὰρ πατρὶ τῶνδʼ Ἀθά-
sections.translation: for instance , men say Athene used to champion their father , and now the citizens of that goddess have saved his children , and checked the insolence of him , whose heart

sections.label: Section 2.925
sections.original: μὸς ἦν πρὸ δίκας βίαιος .
sections.translation: preferred violence to justice . God save me from such arrogance , such greed of soul ! [ Eurystheus is brought in bound .

sections.label: Section 928
sections.original: δέσποινʼ , ὁρᾷς μέν , ἀλλʼ ὅμως εἰρήσεται ,
sections.translation: Mistress , though thine eyes see him , yet will I announce we have brought Eurystheus hither for thy pleasure ,

sections.label: Section 930
sections.original: ἄελπτον ὄψιν , τῷδέ τʼ οὐχ ἧσσον τύχην ·
sections.translation: an unexpected sight , for him no less a chance he ne ’ er foresaw ; for little he thought of ever falling into thy hands , what time he marched from Mycenae with his toil-worn warriors , to sack Athens , thinking himself far above fortune . But a power

sections.label: Section 935
sections.original: δαίμων ἔθηκε , καὶ μετέστησεν τύχην .
sections.translation: divine hath reversed our destinies , changing their position . Now Hyllus and brave Iolaus I left raising an image to Zeus , who routs the foe , for their triumphant victory , whilst they bid me bring this prisoner to thee , wishing to gladden thy heart ; for ’ tis the sweetest

sections.label: Section 940
sections.original: ἥδιστον ἐχθρὸν ἄνδρα δυστυχοῦνθʼ ὁρᾶν .
sections.translation: sight to see a foe fall on evil days after prosperity .

sections.label: Section 941
sections.original: ὦ μῖσος , ἥκεις ; εἷλέ σʼ ἡ Δίκη χρόνῳ ;
sections.translation: Art come , thou hateful wretch ? Hath Justice caught thee then at last ? First , turn thy head this way to me , and endure to look thy enemies in the face , for thou art no more the ruler , but the slave .

sections.label: Section 945
sections.original: ἐκεῖνος εἶ σύ — βούλομαι γὰρ εἰδέναι —
sections.translation: Art [*] thou the man — for this I fain would learn — who didst presume to heap thy insults on my son , who now is where he is , thou miscreant ? What outrage didst thou abstain from putting upon him ? Thou that didst make him go down alive even to Hades ,

sections.label: Section 950
sections.original: ὕδρας λέοντάς τʼ ἐξαπολλύναι λέγων
sections.translation: and wouldst send him with an order to slay hydras and lions ? Thy other evil schemes I mention not , for to tell them were a tedious task for me . Nor did it content thee to venture thus far only ; no ! but from all Hellas wouldst thou drive me and my children ,

sections.label: Section 955
sections.original: ἤλαυνες ἱκέτας δαιμόνων καθημένους ,
sections.translation: heaven ’ s suppliants though we were , grey-heads some of us , and some still tender babes . But here hast thou found men and a free city , that feared not thee . Die in torment must thou , and e ’ en so wilt thou gain in every way , for one death is not

sections.label: Section 960
sections.original: θνῄσκειν σὲ πολλὰ πήματʼ ἐξειργασμένον .
sections.translation: thy due , after all the sorrow thou hast caused .

sections.label: Section 961
sections.original: οὐκ ἔστʼ ἀνυστὸν τόνδε σοι κατακτανεῖν .
sections.translation: Thou mayst not slay him .

sections.label: Section 962
sections.original: ἄλλως ἄρʼ αὐτὸν αἰχμάλωτον εἵλομεν ;
sections.translation: Then have we taken him captive in vain . But say , what law forbids his death ?

sections.label: Section 964
sections.original: τοῖς τῆσδε χώρας προστάταισιν οὐ δοκεῖ .
sections.translation: It is not the will of the rulers of this land .

sections.label: Section 965
sections.original: τί δὴ τόδʼ ; ἐχθροὺς τοισίδʼ οὐ καλὸν κτανεῖν ;
sections.translation: Why , what is this ? Do they not approve of slaying enemies ?

sections.label: Section 966
sections.original: οὐχ ὅντινʼ ἄν γε ζῶνθʼ ἕλωσιν ἐν μάχῃ .
sections.translation: Not such as they have taken alive in battle .

sections.label: Section 967
sections.original: καὶ ταῦτα δόξανθʼ Ὕλλος ἐξηνέσχετο ;
sections.translation: Did Hyllus uphold this decision ?

sections.label: Section 968
sections.original: χρῆν δʼ αὐτόν , οἶμαι , τῇδʼ ἀπιστῆσαι χθονί ;
sections.translation: He , I suppose , ought to have disobeyed the law of the land .

sections.label: Section 969
sections.original: χρῆν τόνδε μὴ ζῆν μηδʼ ὁρᾶν φάος ἔτι .
sections.translation: The prisoner ’ s life ought not to have been spared a moment .

sections.label: Section 970
sections.original: τότʼ ἠδικήθη πρῶτον οὐ θανὼν ὅδε .
sections.translation: It was then that he was wronged , by not being slain at first .

sections.label: Section 971
sections.original: οὐκ οὖν ἔτʼ ἐστὶν ἐν καλῷ δοῦναι δίκην ;
sections.translation: Why , then , he is still in time to pay his penalty .

sections.label: Section 972
sections.original: οὐκ ἔστι τοῦτον ὅστις ἂν κατακτάνοι .
sections.translation: There is no one , who will slay him now .

sections.label: Section 973
sections.original: ἔγωγε · καίτοι φημὶ κἄμʼ εἶναί τινα .
sections.translation: I will ; and yet I count myself someone .

sections.label: Section 974
sections.original: πολλὴν ἄρʼ ἕξεις μέμψιν , εἰ δράσεις τόδε .
sections.translation: Well , thou wilt incur great blame , if thou do this deed .

sections.label: Section 975
sections.original: φιλῶ πόλιν τήνδʼ · οὐδὲν ἀντιλεκτέον .
sections.translation: I love this city well ; that cannot be gainsaid . But since this man hath fallen into my power , no mortal hand shall wrest him from me . Wherefore let who will , call me the woman bold , with thoughts too high for her sex ;

sections.label: Section 980
sections.original: λέξει · τὸ δʼ ἔργον τοῦτʼ ἐμοὶ πεπράξεται .
sections.translation: yet shall this deed be brought to pass by me .
